questions.question: 1. In the iron-carbon phase diagram, what is the maximum carbon content for steel?

explanations.a: This is an incorrect option. This corresponds to the maximum solubility of carbon in ferrite (α-iron) at room temperature, not the upper limit of carbon content in steel.
explanations.b: This is an incorrect option. This is the eutectoid composition of steel (where austenite transforms into pearlite), but steels can contain significantly more carbon than this.
explanations.c: This is the correct option. In the iron–carbon (Fe–C) phase diagram, steel is defined as an alloy of iron containing up to 2.11 wt.% carbon.
explanations.d: This is an incorrect option. This is the carbon content of cementite (Fe₃C) and represents the upper limit of the iron–carbon phase diagram, characteristic of cast iron, not steel.

questions.question: 2. At what temperature does the eutectoid reaction occur in the iron-carbon phase diagram?

explanations.a: This is the correct option. The eutectoid reaction (γ → α + Fe₃C) occurs specifically at 723 °C in the iron–carbon phase diagram.
explanations.b: This is an incorrect option. This temperature corresponds to the α-iron (ferrite) to γ-iron (austenite) transformation in pure iron, not the eutectoid reaction.
explanations.c: This is an incorrect option. This temperature is associated with the eutectic reaction in the Fe–C system (liquid → austenite + cementite), not the eutectoid reaction.
explanations.d: This is an incorrect option. This is close to the peritectic reaction temperature (liquid + δ-ferrite → austenite), unrelated to the eutectoid transformation.

questions.question: 3. Which phase of steel has a body-centered cubic (BCC) crystal structure?

explanations.a: This is the correct option.  The phase of steel with a BCC crystal structure is ferrite.
explanations.b: This is an incorrect option.  Cementite is not a pure iron phase with an orthorhombic structure.
explanations.c: This is an incorrect option.  Austenite has an FCC structure, not BCC.
explanations.d: This is an incorrect option. It is not a single phase. Instead, it is a mixture (lamellar structure) of ferrite (BCC) and cementite.

questions.question: 4. Steel with a carbon content below the eutectoid composition is classified as:?

explanations.a: This is an incorrect option.  Hypereutectoid steels contain more carbon than the eutectoid composition.
explanations.b: This is the correct option. Steels with carbon content below the eutectoid composition are correctly classified as hypoeutectoid steels.
explanations.c: This is an incorrect option.  Cast iron contains a high carbon amount, which is far above the eutectoid composition.
explanations.d: This is an incorrect option.  Martensite refers to a microstructure formed by rapid quenching, not a classification based on carbon content.

questions.question: 5. At what carbon content does steel become classified as hypereutectoid?

explanations.a: This is an incorrect option. Carbon contents above 2.11 wt% fall in the cast iron range, not steel.
explanations.b: This is the correct option. Hypereutectoid steel is defined as steel with carbon content greater than the eutectoid composition (0.76 wt%) but less than 2.11 wt%.
explanations.c: This is an incorrect option. This corresponds to very low-carbon (ultra-low carbon) steel, well below the eutectoid composition, and is classified as hypoeutectoid, not hypereutectoid.
explanations.d: This is an incorrect option.  Steels with carbon content below 0.76 wt% are hypoeutectoid steels, containing ferrite + pearlite.

questions.question: 6. Which type of steel has a microstructure consisting of ferrite and cementite lamellae?

explanations.a: This is an incorrect option. Consists mainly of austenite (FCC), not ferrite–cementite lamellae.
explanations.b: This is an incorrect option. Has a hard, needle-like martensite structure, not lamellae.
explanations.c: This is the correct option. Pearlite is a lamellar (layered) structure made of alternating layers of ferrite and cementite formed during the eutectoid reaction in steel.
explanations.d: This is an incorrect option. Cementite is present as spherical particles, not lamellae.

questions.question: 7. What is the carbon content at the eutectoid point in the Fe–C phase diagram?

explanations.a: This is an incorrect option. This is close to the carbon solubility limit of carbon in ferrite (α-Fe) at the eutectoid temperature, not the eutectoid composition itself.
explanations.b: This is the correct option. The eutectoid composition in the Fe–C phase diagram is 0.76 wt% carbon.
explanations.c: This is an incorrect option. This is the maximum carbon solubility in austenite at the eutectic temperature and marks the boundary between steel and cast iron.
explanations.d: This is an incorrect option. This corresponds to the eutectic composition in the Fe–C diagram, not the eutectoid point.
